data.title: Axon: Don't Pull The Trigger Yet (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
As shown below, it has been nearly a year now since I initiated Axon Enterprise, Inc. (AXON) with a sell rating. While that article was primarily focused on the technical side of things, I found 2025 guidance to be disappointing and the stock to be grossly overvalued. Even though Axon advanced after that article was published, it has now given up all those gains and then some. As of this writing, the stock is down more than 20% over this period while the S&P500 is up over 20%. That is massive underperformance.

With the company having reported earnings just in late February, today I'll be providing a much needed update to see whether the investment thesis has changed.

In the below analysis, it is shown that while the long-term opportunity is large and the Q4 results are overall solid, growth guidance for 2026 may be a little disappointing. Furthermore, the company is facing some risks, such as ICE involvement, and so despite the significant contraction in the valuation, I believe the risk/reward setup for investors isn't attractive enough yet. While I've decided to upgrade Axon, I believe investors should hold off on pulling the trigger for now.

The Opportunity at Hand

Before we look at the numbers specific to their 2025 Q4, let's discuss how big the long-term opportunity is for Axon. As shown in the infographic above, their TAM has now reached $159 billion, and in my view that suggests that the runway for growth is robust. Investors should note that the company saw $2.78 billion worth of revenues for 2025 as a whole, so if they are able to execute and capitalize on the size of this market, prospects are bright. As stated in the infographic, U.S. State & Local TAM is less than 15% penetrated, so the opportunity seems large as the world shifts to the next generation of enforcement equipment. In their investor deck, they highlighted the uptrend in U.S. Public Safety spending, so the market itself is set to continue expanding.

Another thing I wanted to point out is that Axon is, in fact, quite diversified in its offerings. With the company formerly called Taser International, it isn't hard to see why investors mostly associate them with that product. However, as shown in the above infographic, Taser & Training represents just $14 billion of their TAM. On the hardware side of things, Axon now offers advanced solutions like drones and robotics, while on the software side of things they offer services like digital evidence management as well as AI tools. Therefore, investors should recognize that the company is in fact involved with potentially higher-growth opportunities than just their Taser products.

Growth at Multiyear Highs

Let's now get into the results for 2025 Q4. In the quarter, Axon generated revenues of $797 million, representing a growth rate of 39% YoY. They stated that this was "driven by premium software adoption, TASER 10, Axon Body 4 and counter-drone equipment," so they are indeed more than just a taser company. As shown in the chart above, growth has accelerated rather steeply to a multiyear high level. That suggests that business activity is strong and that they seem to be capturing the opportunity that's present in a nice manner. Furthermore, Axon beat analyst expectations by $41.43 million, so the fourth quarter was one that showed clear outperformance. Based on the top line, the company is executing nicely.

Demand is Strong

While revenue growth already tells quite a bit about where demand is currently, the four metrics above show that the strong top-line growth we saw in Q4 could potentially be sustained. In terms of bookings and ARR, you can see that there was a considerable jump for both metrics YoY as a sign that future business activity will be strong. The fact that nearly all revenues are from customers on subscription plans makes their revenues durable. Lastly, perhaps most importantly, Axon's net revenue retention has continued to improve YoY. This is a key metric as it shows that customers are liking the user experience. Axon, therefore, has a solid value proposition, and of course that is an encouraging sign.

Margins & Profitability

On the right, you can see that in annual terms, Axon's adjusted EBITDA margin is clearly moving in the right direction. Therefore, in the grand scheme of things, profitability seems to be improving. For Q4 specifically, there are some slightly mixed numbers. In the fourth quarter, adjusted gross margin contracted by 210 basis points to 61.1% as the company cited tariffs and product mix. Even for a law enforcement equipment company, tariffs have eroded cost control and dented profitability. While this isn't good news, do note that they still managed to report an adjusted EBITDA margin of 25.9% for Q4. That is a considerable improvement from the year-ago quarter's 24.6%, so Axon has made up for that gross margin weakness with nice corporate efficiency, it seems.

Outlook: 2026 & Beyond

Axon provided some guidance for full-year 2026. For revenue, the range shown above would be a deceleration from what we saw in 2025. That is quite disappointing given the large TAM that was outlined earlier and the strong demand metrics we went over. With bookings having grown by 43% YoY, I would have expected stronger top line projections. Therefore, execution relative to the opportunity at hand may be less than perfect. For adjusted EBITDA margin, things are projected to stay unchanged, so despite the tariff-related gross margin pressure, Axon's core profitability is set to remain resilient moving forward.

In addition to these 2026 estimates, the company also gave targets for 2028. By then, Axon sees revenues of around $6 billion. If you pull out your calculator, you can see that their target potentially hints at continued growth in the high twenties percentage range for 2027 and 2028. Therefore, while the slowdown from 2025 isn't desirable, the company is still in a phase of sustained, resilient growth. For adjusted EBITDA margin, they are targeting 28% for 2028, so moving forward, the company expects to continue improving efficiency and profitability.

Risks

One of the key risks for Axon is its involvement with ICE in the United States. While the company's moonshot goal is to reduce gun-related deaths between the police and the public, the use of their equipment and solutions for a highly controversial mission sets the company up for reputational risk.

In mid-March, Axios Denver reported that Axon is the company that the mayor's administration has chosen to take over their automated license plate reader system. The only problem is that Axon's strong ties with the Department of Homeland Security mean that the company has a relationship with ICE. In Denver, there has reportedly been public denouncing of ICE tactics and policies, so it is clear why there has been some controversy. In my view, this is a clear example of the reputational risk Axon is facing under this Trump Administration.

Valuation Much More Reasonable

As shown in the chart above, Axon's forward P/E ratio has contracted by a massive amount since mid last year. Readings of over 120 weren't unheard of, so at the current level of 55.27, the valuation seems to be at much more reasonable levels now. I would say this may help to justify a revision to a hold rating.

In today's analysis, it was shown that the long-term opportunity is sizeable as the TAM makes it clear that the company has room to grow. Q4 results were encouraging as top-line growth accelerated to multiyear highs and adjusted EBITDA margin expanded. However, while demand metrics were robust, 2026 guidance was shown to be a little disappointing. The margin target for 2028 is quite encouraging, but growth may remain in just the high 20s percentage range. I expected better exploitation of the large market opportunity moving forward, and with risks like tariff cost pressures and ICE involvement present, I would say that the contraction in the valuation hasn't been completely unjustified.

At the end of the day, a forward P/E of over 50 is not particularly cheap for a company that's projecting top-line growth in the high twenties for 2026. That said, given the long-term potential of the business and the huge correction in the valuation, I do believe the risk/reward is now more or less neutral.

Conclusion

Nearly a year ago, I initiated Axon at a sell rating, and ultimately my bearish thesis has played out quite well. Today, I have decided that it's time to upgrade the stock to a hold as the fundamental setup is around neutral. A large long-term opportunity and strong Q4 results are met with mixed guidance and some notable risks. The valuation has come down but still isn't particularly cheap, so the risk/reward seems quite balanced currently. Therefore, I believe investors should hold off on pulling the trigger at this point despite the fact that the stock is more attractive now than it has been.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 03/31/26
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: EVT: Time For Active Management For Equity Positioning
data.description:
The Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (EVT) is a closed-end fund designed to provide investors with diversified equity exposure with the goal of generating high distributable income. Managed equity strategies may be exceptionally appealing during periods of significant market disruption, particularly as major indices face exogenous pressure, making successful stock selection a highly valued skill.

With mounting uncertainty regarding the global supply chain disruptions raised from the Iranian war, I believe investors should take into consideration managed CEFs like EVT as a component of a diversified portfolio strategy. Given the near-term risks in the market and comparable historical performance, I am recommending EVT with a Hold rating.

Investment Thesis

EVT was designed to invest in value-oriented, dividend-paying stocks that may provide tax-advantaged dividend income as part of its investment objective, presenting an appealing opportunity given the state of the broader equity market. Prior to the Iranian war, I believe the markets were heavily shifting in favor of value over growth stocks as investors began questioning the quality of the AI theme and whether the high premiums appropriately priced the stocks, as well as the software stock disruption with the emergence of the theme that AI may replace traditional enterprise software.

While I don’t believe this second factor will necessarily be meaningfully impactful to the market given how integrated enterprise software has become, tethered together with custom applications, I do expect AI to disrupt the market with respect to being more of a companion feature. Granted, this is highly selective, as a CRM or ERP company will be less likely disrupted when compared to a creative suite.

I believe the recent events in the Middle East added fuel to the flame, adding pressure to oil prices and uncertainty towards global supply chains, as a large proportion of the world’s oil and natural gas comes out of the Middle East through the Strait of Hormuz. With LNG capacity coming offline resulting from missile strikes, the global gas supply may be disrupted for years to come. The economic impact was higher oil and natural gas prices.

Overall, I believe this is one of the leading factors influencing the equity markets, adding pressure and significant uncertainty.

With the global market being disrupted, I believe there is no greater time than now to be a stock picker or offload the risk to professional asset managers that can actively manage their equity strategy through diversification. One of the values EVT provides is that the fund provides value and dividend exposure as well as broad sector diversification, enabling the portfolio management team to dynamically position the portfolio to mitigate risks related to the war in the Middle East.

The risk at hand is that EVT may be exposed to a challenging market environment. The top holding within the strategy is Micron (MU) at 3.96%. While I have reason to believe that Micron is well positioned for growth given the successful sales of high-bandwidth memory for AI accelerator chips, the company may face certain headwinds relating to the war as the global supply chain for helium is being disrupted, which may impact the rate of chip production in the coming quarters. Accordingly, Qatar supplies a third of the world’s helium, a major bottleneck given that the Ras Laffan facility was struck by missiles and experienced extensive damage.

Helium is used during the etching process when fabricating semiconductors, ensuring that the silicon doesn’t overheat and crack.

This challenge could significantly impact companies whose growth is underpinned on sourcing AI accelerators to expand their data center scale, including Amazon (AMZN), which is held at 2.96%, Alphabet (GOOG) at 2.54%, and to a lesser degree, Meta Platforms (META) at 1.76%.

Aside from the sector risk involved, I believe the value- and dividend-oriented investment strategy can provide investors with an appealing proposition as a way to seek out investment durability over high growth potential. While I expect value and dividend stocks to experience similar negative pricing action as a result of the market sell-off, I do not believe that the sell-off will be as extensive when compared to growth-oriented stocks that may trade at higher premiums, providing investors with some cushion to soften the blow.

About Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund

EVT was launched on September 30, 2003 by Eaton Vance. EVT has a total expense ratio of 227bps, 103bps of which are management fees. EVT presently has $2.1b in net assets, inclusive of 18.1% leverage applied to the share assets. Leverage can be appealing for income-focused strategies as it may provide investors with amplified exposure to distributable income, though it may also increase risk exposure.

EVT is diversified across 75 holdings, 79.30% of which are equities and 19.43% fixed income & preferred stock. The strategy is primarily allocated to US equities at 92.99%, with some exposure in Europe and Latin America. The strategy is most heavily weighted in the financial sector, making up 31.77% of the portfolio weight. Other sector concentrations include industrials at 12.18%, health care at 9.22%, and information technology at 9.13%.

EVT pays out a monthly distribution with a forward rate of $1.98/share, yielding 8.31%. EVT’s year-to-date distributions have primarily derived from net realized long-term capital gains [71.5%] and net investment income [16.2%], suggesting that the distribution was largely influenced by stock selection and rotation and, to a lesser degree, dividend income.

Comparing performance, EVT has historically performed in the middle of the pack, with the Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX) being the top performer over most historical holding periods. ADX also charges a relatively lower fee to investors with an expense ratio of 56bps, far below peer strategies.

ADX also presents an appealing distribution at 8.68%, just above EVT’s distribution rate of 8.31%. Given the historical performance, fees, and robust distribution, ADX may be considered as an alternative investment strategy for those seeking equity income and growth.

Risks Related to EVT

EVT is an actively managed, income-oriented investment strategy designed to maximize total returns for investors by investing in value- and dividend-paying stocks, exposing investors to certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. Investors in EVT may be exposed to manager risk and the portfolio management team’s ability to appropriately select and weight securities. With emerging events in the Middle East and the major disruption to the global supply chain, I believe the portfolio management team will be especially tested as to their ability to mitigate risk relating to global trade. US equities may continue to face pressure from high oil prices and the continuation of the Iranian war, potentially negatively impacting equity strategies.

Final Thoughts

The equity market may face significant pressure resulting from the Iranian war and the disruption to the global supply chain, making security selection a crucial skill in creating a successful portfolio strategy. I believe the thematic approach to value and dividend security selection can be appealing for investors as software and AI are rotated out of favor, though EVT presents significant exposure to the AI theme through its investments in the hyperscalers and chip producers. When comparing historical performance, the low-cost CEF ADX has outperformed over most historical periods, presenting an appealing alternative for those seeking dividend income.

Given these factors, I am recommending EVT with a Hold rating.

data.title: Barrick Mining: Structural Tailwinds Make It A Golden Opportunity
data.description:
Introduction

I haven’t had the chance to review Barrick Mining Corporation (B) yet, while covering peers in the mining industry that tend to have a broader asset base. I will now take the plunge following the rally in gold prices and the ensuing normalization despite macro risks ticking up.

There are a lot of inherent catalysts to the firm’s valuation, such as gold prices, record FCF and the potential incoming IPO of Barrick’s NA gold assets. I will go through them to see if B will continue to offer strong value to shareholders.

Current Dynamics

I’ll start with the macro backdrop that has pushed gold to rally and falter with the Iran situation. Gold started the year at around $4,384 per ounce and pushed forward to nearly $5,500 an ounce. It now came back to $4,532 as of the last available prices. The current price drivers are structural in my mind, rather than simply speculative, as central bank demand is roughly 585 tonnes per quarter through 2026, as sovereign wealth funds are diversifying away from dollar-denominated assets. More so, the US-China trade tensions have pushed the Chinese government to diversify away from US treasuries toward gold. The PBOC has been a main actor in this, with its 15th consecutive month of reserve increases bringing the total official gold holdings to 2,308 tonnes. Regulation also played in this facet as Chinese institutional investors are now allowed to allocate up to 1% of their assets directly into bullion.

Furthermore, many believe that gold should have continued its rise in price, considering the increased macro risks linked to the Iran conflict, but prices have fallen sharply and have been highly volatile ever since. The downward pressure is essentially linked to the rise in crude prices due to the Strait of Hormuz, which is fueling global inflation fears. This would thus translate in a higher interest rate environment or even increase rates to combat the shock, pushing treasury yields higher, thus increasing the demand for US treasuries, reducing the demand for non-yielding assets like gold. The systemic shock of a war also pushed investors to sell assets in order to go back to cash and wait it out.

On Barrick Gold’s side, the conflict has had a direct impact on the Reko Diq copper-gold project in Pakistan, which has seen reduced activity and spending.

Barrick has also decided to spin off its NA assets into a single entity, which is a drastic pivot from the conglomerate model that has kept share prices down compared to pure geographical plays like Agnico Eagle. This will basically reduce the geopolitical risks of international operations in countries like Mali and Papua New Guinea. By doing so, B is hoping that this will force a sum-of-the-parts re-rating recognizing the inherent value of the Tier-1 assets.

The new company will be a concentration of the world’s highest-quality gold assets, with the centerpiece being the participation in the Nevada Gold Mines, which is the world’s largest gold mining joint venture. NGM has seen a strong performance in 2025 thanks to its operational synergies that have led to a 25% sequential increase in production from the Carlin complex. NewCo will also own the Formile project, which has been dubbed the gold discovery of the century and acts as the main organic growth engine for the new company. Recent drilling has doubled the inferred resources to nearly 8MM ounces at world-leading grades. NewCo is also expected to be a high-yield cash machine.

The IPO will be a float of 10 to 15% of the total shares while retaining a 90% ownership stake, though the valuation is currently threatened by a legal firestorm with Newmont (NEM).

Valuation

Despite the rise in gold prices, the company is relatively cheap, even the cheapest among its peers, with its P/E GAAP of 13.16, below Newmont’s 15.98 and Agnico Eagle Mines' (AEM) at 21.83. Though despite the high profitability levels, the firm has seen its FCF yield hang at a relatively medium level at 3.3%. The company also has a slim dividend yield of 2.19%, that has grown tremendously well following the rise in gold prices, with a 5YR CAGR of 20.69%. I also wanted to dive deeper outside of simple financial sand, that’s why I did a sum of the parts analysis and a scenario analysis, taking into account the IPO of NewCo.

As you can see, the SOTP model would imply a fair value of $43.02 which would only be an upside of 11.5%, despite the IPO of the NewCo. But the bull case mentioned in the scenario analysis would potentially give a 13x premium IPO multiple, thus making the fair value of legacy Barrick’s mining share surge to $50.18 per share, which I see as highly possible if this environment persists.

Growth-wise, I’m bullish on Copper, which has been deemed a multi-decade strategic vector by the company. Management has projected that copper will amount to nearly 40% of the company’s EBITDA by 2030, thanks to the global energy transition tailwind as well as AI-related infrastructure. B has guided for a production of 190,000 to 220,000 tonnes of copper with an AISC of between $3.45 and $3.75 per pound. The company has also restored a normal situation in Mali with the Loulo-Gounkoto development, which will produce around 260K to 290K ounces for 2026, which is a drastic reintegration of production.

Risks

The biggest risk would be the current legal firestorm with Newmont, which is the junior partner in the NGM JV, alleging that there is a case of systematic mismanagement and resource piracy as Barrick allegedly diverted resources to its Fourmile project. Newmont is planning to block the NewCo IPO and it could further escalate in a possible buyout, dismantling the cornerstone of the NewCo valuation.

Barrick’s overall outlook has been digested as a transition year rather than straight growth, which has shocked some investors. B will produce less gold this year as it has guided at 2.9 to 3.25MM ounces due to maintenance and sequencing, though this would mean that Barrick will not fully capitalize on the highest gold prices in history.

Conclusion

If you want exposure to gold, I would always recommend buying a mining company in the field rather than owning the asset outright, considering the working asset aspect of holding shares. I believe that the recent drawback in stock offers investors a strong opportunity to get in, banking on a ton of structural tailwinds for the gold industry as a whole, and mainly Barrick Mining. I rate the company a Buy.

data.title: Janus International Group Gets More Attractive The Cheaper It Gets (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
In early December of last year, I detailed how shares of Janus International Group (JBI) had taken a hit since I had downgraded the stock from a ‘buy’ to a ‘hold’. That decline amounted to 38.9% at a time when the S&P 500 was up 6.3%. This was a substantial disappointment for those who own the stock most certainly. However, what many viewed as pain, I argued was opportunity. While I acknowledged that there was weakness in the space and that this weakness would likely continue for the near term, I also made the case that shares were cheap enough to justify a bullish outlook. Since then, however, the stock has fallen another 13.4%. This compares to the 4.5% drop that the market experienced.

Some investors might conclude, after such a substantial decline, that maybe there is something broken about the company. I would argue, however, that the market is simply overreacting. This year, as an example, management expects some rather attractive growth on both the top and bottom lines. And based on how the stock is priced on a forward basis, it is difficult to be anything other than bullish about it. I wouldn't be surprised if fundamental performance ends up being weaker than anticipated simply because I am pessimistic about the state of the economy. But for those with a long-term investment horizon, Janus International Group seems to be a very clear candidate for consideration. In fact, I would even go so far as to say that upgrading it to a ‘strong buy’ makes sense here.

Upside is warranted

The only new data that investors have from Janus International Group covers through the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue amounted to $226.3 million. That's 1.9% below the 230 point $8 million that the company reported the previous year. This pain was driven primarily by the company’s self-storage revenue that comes from new construction. Year over year, revenue plummeted 8.1% from $96.7 million to $88.9 million.

This might seem surprising. After all, as the image above illustrates, data from Extra Space Storage (EXR) shows that, from 1987 through 2023, the US has gone from 2.7% of all households using self storage to 12.6% of them using it. Another big competitor in the space, Public Storage (PSA) mentioned that while there exists a hefty 2.7 billion square feet in this fragmented industry, that growth is continuing, but only slowly. In 2026, as an example, they believe that competitive new supply growth will be just 2%. That's down from the 2.5% seen back in 2025 and the 5% back in 2019.

Weak housing market conditions have proven to be problematic for the space. Extra Space Storage, as an example, said that only 32% of demand for self storage comes from a lack of space. But 55% is because people are moving and they need temporary storage space for their possessions. But because of high interest rates and inflationary pressures that have made homes unaffordable for a large chunk of Americans, construction of new properties has fallen.

In January of this year, for instance, housing completions were 7.5% below what they were a year earlier. Housing starts had risen 9.5%, but building permits were down 5.8% year over year. The surge in housing starts is almost certainly because of home builders trying to capture revenue before cancellation rates rise and in order to boost cash flow in the near term. But the fact that building permits and housing completions are both down is disconcerting to say the least.

This is not to say that the entire self storage side of the business was worse. We should not ignore its R3 category of it, which involves the restoration, rebuilding, and replacement, of self storage facilities. These do need upkeep after all. According to management, almost 65% of the facilities in the US are more than 20 years old. As players in the space consolidate, the combined entities look for ways to boost returns. This often takes the form of additional growth. Cost cutting is also a factor. But making sure that facilities are up to date is also a great way to boost overall appeal for those looking for self storage solutions. Revenue for this part of the company grew 12.7% from $56.7 million to $63.9 million. And it is likely that management will continue to focus on growth for this space.

Outside of the self storage category, the firm does have its ‘commercial and other’ space. This includes a wide array of offerings, such as the metal decking product line owned by BETCO that was recently expanded, as well as a new high security swing door. The company’s ASTA business, meanwhile, rolled out its high performance offering during the quarter. But that was not enough to prevent sales from falling by 5%, with management attributing that largely to softness and demand for commercial sheet doors.

The first chart in this article shows that while revenue for the company did drop, some profitability metrics improved. Net income went from $0.3 million to $7.1 million. And on an adjusted basis, we got a rise from $13.5 million to $15.6 million. Operating cash flow did plummet from $51.4 million to $24.8 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a rise from $20.3 million to $33.5 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $34.6 million to $37.2 million.

The chart above shows financial performance for 2025 compared to 2024. Because of the weaknesses that I mentioned already, revenue did drop. But there is some good news here. Even though management acknowledged that certain market headwinds remain in place, the company continues to focus on growth by means of acquisition activities. This included its purchase recently of Kiwi II, which was a strategic move by the company to grow its exterior solutions business. The expectation here is that these kinds of maneuvers will push revenue up for 2026 to between $940 million and $980 million. Between $90 million and $100 million of that is associated with this acquisition alone. And when you add on top of this certain cost cutting initiatives, like the 2025 cost reduction program that targeted $10 million in annual pre-tax cost savings, it's not surprising that management expects EBITDA to come in at between $165 million and $185 million.

If management can hit that target, it would likely mean adjusted operating cash flow of $159.1 million and adjusted net profits $86.8 million. Using these estimates, as well as the results of the company achieved for 2025, we can see in the chart below how shares are priced at the moment. These are actually very attractive multiples if you ask me. What's more, compared to other similar enterprises, Janus International Group looks quite cheap. In that chart, I stacked it up against five such businesses. On a price to earnings basis, it ended up being the cheapest of the group. But when it came to the other two valuation approaches, only one of the five firms ended up being cheaper than it is.

Takeaway

All of the data that's available right now indicates to me that while organic performance for Janus International Group might be challenged this year, acquisition oriented expansion is enough to justify bullishness. Shares of the business are incredibly cheap, and I firmly believe in its industry in the long run. Because of this, I firmly believe that upgrading it to a ‘strong buy’ is the appropriate move here.

data.title: Shake Shack: Accelerating Growth And Cash Flow Inflection Support Re-Rating
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I first covered Shake Shack (SHAK), I highlighted their solid foundation, strong brand, industry-leading store-level returns, and robust growth despite the ongoing macro headwinds.

As their aggressive expansion is accelerating and profitability is improving, SHAK remains a Buy, with the current valuation reflecting a significant discount given the company's long-term potential.

Aggressive Expansion Accelerates

SHAK reported mixed Q4 results, with a beat on EPS despite missing the market's revenue estimates by a bit, while restaurant-level profit margins remained solid despite the ongoing macro weakness and pressure, with the free cash flow continuing its inflection, reaching ~$56.51 million in 2025 (compared to $35.66 million in 2024 and -$14.03 million in 2023).

Meanwhile, their aggressive expansion advanced on both fronts, developing domestically and internationally by adding 45 new company-operated stores and 40 licensed ones (with a total of 5 closures), growing to 659 stores at the end of 2025 compared to 579 in 2024.

As for the guidance, SHAK sees an accelerated growth rate in new openings, expecting between 55 and 60 company-operated openings and 40 and 45 licensed openings in 2026, with a slight improvement in restaurant-level profit margin on top.

Meanwhile, the three-year financial targets see low teens growth rates in revenue and system-wide unit growth with at least 50 bps growth per year in margins, which is great for a company that's developing so fast, potentially leading to significant bottom-line growth that would outpace top-line.

Financially, based on SHAK's latest report, they remain in a strong position, with current assets above their current liabilities and a manageable amount of debt (~$250 million worth of 0% convertible notes issued by their parent, SSE Holdings, due in March 2028), going for an asset-light business model given the operating leases, which allows the kind of growth they're delivering while also making it easier for them to close underperforming stores, as opposed to owning the actual building.

Macro Weakness Potentially Getting Worse

To update the idea mentioned in the previous coverage, the consumer environment continued to show significant signs of weakness, and when people reportedly go for trade-offs even for the most basic necessities such as food, it shouldn't be a surprise when what's ultimately an expensive fast-food chain like SHAK is not doing so well, especially when they can also be hurt by tariffs (a factor that recently improved thanks to the Supreme Court's decision) and the falling discretionary budgets.

Especially following the ongoing Iran conflict leading to historic jumps in oil, gas, fertilizer, etc. prices and its potential to spread into a global slowdown (or worse), it's not hard to see why a business like SHAK is at risk.

However, since this weakness is explained to some degree by this environment (years before the Iran conflict), we should also keep in mind the potential to see improvements, especially if Marco Rubio's recent statements are true - even though Trump reportedly wanting to "take the oil of Iran" now and Iran's counterattacks continuing to deteriorate the energy infrastructure of its neighbors, with the risk of escalations rising as the US is sending more troops to the region while the Houthis reportedly joined this conflict with an attack against Israel a couple of days ago, adding pressure and uncertainty surrounding a wider-scale conflict.

Looking at it from a longer-term perspective, we have a solid business with a healthy balance sheet that's still advancing on their expansion even during this environment, potentially accelerating their domestic and even international expansion going forward.

However, although an international expansion adds long-term potential, we should keep in mind that it's not some magical action that adds growth overnight, especially since SHAK goes for franchising internationally instead of operating these locations themselves. Other than the costs of building a supply chain that would allow them to optimize margins, it's important for them to find the right balance between affordability and quality filtering when it comes to take rates/royalties.

Although they highlighted the 20.2% increase in licensing revenue during 2025, with the opening of 40 new licensed Shacks, dividing the $54.1 million in licensing revenue by the ~$837 million in Licensed Shack sales (~$2.23 billion system-wide sales minus $1.39 billion company-operated Shack sales), we get to a ~6.46% royalty rate, which is high. This can expose their franchisees/partners to more market weakness during environments like today, affecting their growth potential and performance.

Something a fellow Seeking Alpha user mentioned in the comments of the previous coverage is the duality of delivery apps. I talked about this aspect of pizza chains before - which were the "ultimate" food delivery product back in the day - and it's true that this can be both a positive for the company since it allows them to tap into a much wider range of customers, but it also does this for everyone else, making it tougher for SHAK to compete, especially during a weak environment.

Ultimately, these delivery platforms basically level the playing field and can even push premium fast-casual brands into the risk of losing their in-store identity, instead reducing them to just another delivery option.

The company is also rapidly adapting to potential health-consciousness trends, launching their Good Fit Menu not long ago, with GLP-1-friendly choices alongside their We Really Cook campaign to spotlight their quality and cook-to-order food, with their CEO highlighting the following during their Q4 Earnings Call:

Lastly, we introduced our Good Fit Menu, featuring a new way to enjoy Shake Shack and start the new year on a healthy note. The Good Fit Menu items meet the current market demand for differing dietary preferences, including high-protein serving sizes. We have always made these items. We simply package them up and merchandise them as a timely, relevant additional sales layer for our business, a great example of our ability to drive sales growth without significant operational or supply chain disruption.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, we're talking about a company that's highly dependent on the consumer, meaning that trends and the current weakness that can get even worse have clear effects on them, despite their relatively solid performance and accelerated growth rates.

At the same time, competition remains very significant, and the company is just one small chain in an ocean of legacy brands, some valued even above hundreds of billions, making it very hard to compete while also having to deal with the macro pressures keeping your quality, improving margins, and growing at the same time with a high risk of seeing disappointments push the market into taxing their valuation even more.

Valuation

For Shake Shack, to highlight their pivot into cash flow growth and relatively light capital intensity, I'm going to start from their $224.355 million in net cash from operations after adding back the $2 million net interest expenses (the goal here is to get to the FCF to Firm for the Enterprise Value), which I'll assume can grow at a 25% CAGR over the first 5 years (which is below their ~30.6% 5-year average growth rate and the market's EPS expectations), while the $165.85 million in CAPEX would grow at a 5% CAGR during the same period.

After this, I'll estimate that the FCF can grow at a 12.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years given their significant potential to expand both domestically and internationally, with a 3% terminal growth rate given the premium and ability to grow in higher-growth regions in the future, as well as an elevated 12% WACC (~14% cost of equity, ~6.5% cost of debt), taking into account the $28.42 million in non-controlling interests and $526.3 million in net debt, as well as 41.83 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an enterprise value of $5.39 billion, for an equity value of $4.84 billion and an intrinsic value of $115.67 per share, which is quite well above the current $81.32 despite using a relatively high WACC, with the market's estimates being even more conservative.

Conclusion

SHAK remains a Buy, backed by their significant expansion potential and accelerated profitability as the company is developing, with a solid foundation and a strong brand behind it.

There are several risks to keep in mind given the industry and their expansion plans, but the current valuation already seems to take a lot of them into account and tax the stock significantly, with an overall solid risk-reward at these levels.

data.title: Universal Music Group: Valuation Has Gotten Cheaper While Fundamentals Have Improved
data.description:
Summary

I gave a buy rating to Universal Music Group (UMGNF) previously in August, as the long-term upside had improved. Back then, underlying fundamentals were sound: subscription revenue growth remained healthy, ad-supported demand was also good, and music publishing was also scaling. For this update, while the share price has been beaten down, I reiterate my buy rating as Streaming 2.0 is now rolling out nicely, core subscription growth remains healthy even before the full benefit shows up, and the Downtown deal makes the growth story broader than before.

Organic Demand and Streaming 2.0

In my last write-up, a big part of the bull case relied on Streaming 2.0 eventually becoming a real growth driver. My argument was that it could lift ARPU, support stronger subscription growth, and improve the economics between UMG and the DSPs. Very encouragingly, this has played out as I expected. Management said it reached Streaming 2.0 agreements with two additional global DSP partners, Spotify (SPOT) and YouTube, and was clear that the rate benefit should start showing up in FY2026. This is very clear proof that UMGNF can make this work, and this should give the market confidence that Streaming 2.0 is no longer just a concept; it is working.

An important point here is that the core subscription business is still strong even before the Streaming 2.0 benefits come through. For those that have missed the earnings, Q4 2025 subscription revenue grew 7.7% in constant currency [CC] and 9.6% on a like-for-like basis. A positive datapoint to show the strength of organic growth is that management said UMG has now delivered more than 8% subscription growth for six straight quarters, with little help from Streaming 2.0 so far. I believe that should give investors more confidence that the organic demand base has not deteriorated at all. If anything, it is solid, and the Streaming 2.0 benefits should easily push this >10% in due time. For what it's worth, it was also noted that six of the top ten markets, including the US, delivered high-single-digit or double-digit subscription growth, so there are upsides to the organic growth.

Broadened Growth Story

I would also say that the equity story is broader now. The main idea in my previous update was that UMGNF could get more value from its existing catalog. I still think that is true. But it was great to see UMG is also building more ways to grow. The Downtown acquisition is a good example. The important thing here is not that it added more scale, but that it expands UMGNF's reach to >5,000 business clients and >4 million creators across 145 countries. This is not the typical deal where UMGNF simply buys a catalog; this deal pushes UMGNF deeper into the services side of music (like music creation, distribution, publishing, marketing, etc.).

My inference is that this gives UMGNF access to a much larger customer base beyond the artists and catalogs it already owns, especially for independent artists. The latter point is more important than it looks because it gives UMGNF deeper exposure to a part of the entire music market where it can grow by finding and growing creators and labels, not just by signing or acquiring them. From an investment angle, you can see this as UMGNF building an ecosystem where it can source the best "startup company" (the independent artist) when it is still unknown and "invest" early. This effectively diversifies UMGNF's reliance on already successful artists and also gives it another source of growth.

AI Impact is Not That Big

One debate point that is heavily under scrutiny recently is the AI risk. The debate is whether AI-generated music would eventually dilute streams, weaken UMGNF's ability to charge royalties, and eventually reduce the value of UMGNF's owned catalog.

My framing on this is that it is not as bad as it seems. In fact, I think UMGNF is well positioned on this. So far, per UMGNF's presentation, AI music engagement by consumers is still minimal, which means any near-term AI dilution risk is not material. As you can see in the slide above, the top 10 AI acts of 2025 accounted for <0.015% of total streams for the top 50,000 artists in the US. Organic stream demand for AI is also below 0.5% of platform consumption. I am not saying that this risk disappears entirely, but from the data we are seeing, it does not support the idea that AI is already hurting UMGNF's unit economics and growth in any meaningful way.

Valuation

I get why the market has punished UMGNF's stock. Earnings growth has decelerated to just 7% in FY2025, and the US listing catalyst is off the table for now. But I believe this view is too myopic. The main growth drivers for UMGNF, like the Streaming 2.0 initiatives and core organic subscription growth, are moving in the right direction. The Downtown deal also broadens UMGNF's ability to grow. Fundamentally, I don't see any major issues. My view is that this leaves UMG with a credible path back to mid-teens earnings growth, and if that happens, I think a return to 28x NTM PE is reasonable since that was where the stock traded when earnings growth last reached 15% in June 2024. I see a potential upside of >2x.

Investment Risk

The Downtown deal adds scale and reach, but note that it is margin dilutive at the start (Downtown is a lower-margin business). UMGNF is already dealing with headwinds from lower-margin activities such as Virgin Music and physical sales; if these lower-margin activities grow faster than the core subscription business, earnings growth may stay pressured on a headline basis, and that would make it harder for the stock to re-rate quickly.

Conclusion

I still see UMGNF as a buy. Streaming 2.0 is becoming a real growth driver, organic subscription trends remain strong, and Downtown gives UMG more ways to grow. The AI overhang also looks much less severe than the market fears today. If UMG continues to execute, I believe the current valuation has meaningful upside.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Undercovered Dozen: Power Solutions, Kraft Heinz, W. P. Carey, And More
data.description:
The Undercovered Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser-covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this provides ideas and inspires discussion among the community.

Today, we're looking at articles published between March 20 and March 26.

Take a look at what these less-covered stocks might hold for you. And please join the conversation below to share what you think: Are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?

Power Solutions: Market Overreacting To Temporary Margin Pressure, Reiterating Strong Buy

Research Wise | Strong Buy

Exiting 2025, the provider of power systems to the AI infrastructure, Power Solutions International (PSIX), reported a double beat in its fourth quarter, with continued momentum across the top line. However, the company's margins remain under pressure during the quarter due to certain drivers, which also impacted the bottom line during Q4. In my view, with significant investment in AI infrastructure across North America, the demand environment for the company's products and solutions, particularly in its key power system segment, should remain robust, supporting sustained top line growth moving into 2026.

Read more here.

Kraft Heinz: A Turnaround Story Coming With A ~14% Free Cash Flow Yield

David Desjardins | Buy

The creation of Kraft Heinz (KHC) as a single entity goes back to July 2015, when the ~$45 billion merger between Kraft Foods and H.J. Heinz finally closed. Even by today's standard, it is still one of the largest transactions in the history of the consumer staples industry, with high-profile investors like Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) and the private equity firm 3G Capital having invested an additional $10 billion in the equity of the combined entity. Coincidentally, the largest deal in the industry's history before the Kraft Heinz merger was the $23 billion acquisition of Heinz orchestrated by the same players in 2013, namely Warren Buffett at Berkshire Hathaway and the Brazilian Jorge Paulo Lemann at 3G Capital.

Read more here.

A Pair Trade Opportunity By Annaly Capital Preferreds

Arbitrage Trader | Hold

When market conditions show increased uncertainty and the associated high volatility, we tend to look for safer trading/investment ideas. Pair trades among similar securities of the same issuer are a prime example of such an approach, as they offer virtually no credit risk. With this short article, we'll have a look at Annaly Capital Management (NLY) and a pair trade opportunity, represented by its preferred stocks. The Series G preferred stock of the company (NLY.PR.G) has been overvalued compared to the other floating rate preferreds of the company. Now, with the falling equity market and the rising benchmark yields, we see an increased probability for a widening of the credit spreads and a potential for NLY-G to be fairly priced to its brothers.

Read more here.

W. P. Carey: I'm Buying Into The Turnaround

Louis Gerard | Buy

After covering REITs like Realty Income (O) and VICI Properties (VICI), I want to set my sights on W. P. Carey (WPC), which has potentially one of the most intriguing stories in the REIT sector, following its office portfolio divestiture and its dividend cut that has pushed the market to highly punish the stock. With that in mind, let’s dive into one of the best opportunities in the space.

Read more here.

RSPA: Equal-Weighted S&P 500 Index Portfolio Generating 9%+ Yield

Nick Ackerman | Hold

The Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Income Advantage ETF (RSPA) is a newer fund that focuses on a more balanced approach within its portfolio. The fund replicates the entire S&P 500 Equal Weight Index, holding all 500 names. The fund then replicates a call-writing overlay strategy. However, they don't actually write the options themselves; instead, they utilize equity-linked notes (ELNs).

With these come some unique differences from some of its call-writing peers. Unfortunately, some of these are downsides rather than positives, in my opinion. That makes RSPA quite an interesting fund for an income investor seeking more diversified exposure relative to the S&P 500 Index call writing funds with heavy concentrations in technology stocks. Though with the downsides of ELNs, I'd be less enthusiastic.

Read more here.

AGNC Investment: Navigating The Fed Pause

Forward Analytics | Buy

Investing in mortgage rate REITs has never been an easy investment, but the early stages of 2026 have brought significant headwinds. What seemed like a promising year for Agency MBS, with mortgage rates briefly falling slightly below 6% for the first time since 2022, was short-lived. It lasted until the US and Israel launched joint airstrikes against Iran. With the ongoing geopolitical tensions, the outlook is uncertain, making what was never an easy investment even more complex.

In the middle of these turbulent waters stands AGNC Investment Corp. (AGNC), which is the leading Agency MBS REIT in the US. For this company, the uncertain macroeconomic climate is double-edged. On one hand, volatile rates amid the ongoing war imply short-term spread volatility and a pause in the easing cycle, which had gone below 6%. Looking at it from the other perspective, this company, to me, seems to be built to weather such storms.

Read more here.

Kraken Robotics: Iran War Signals That The Next Arms Race Is Underwater

Sarfatti Investment Research | Strong Buy

The emergence of critical infrastructure located underwater, such as fiber-optic cables, energy pipelines, and offshore power cables, has created a new dimension of warfare, creating a need for advanced undersea defense capabilities. Further abetting the case for more advanced undersea defense capabilities is the 2026 Iran War, which is being waged over the economic importance of the Strait of Hormuz. I am initiating coverage on Kraken Robotics (KRKNF) with a Strong Buy rating. I believe that KRKNF is a compelling investment opportunity for those looking to capitalize on their strong technological leadership in the undersea defense technology sector, early exposure to Anduril, and the significant shift in this defense market backdrop. Furthermore, KRKNF provides solutions beyond their positioning in defense, overlooked by many investors.

Read more here.

2 Reasons Why Today A Portfolio Needs A 4x On Dividends: The Case For QDPL

Financial Serenity | Buy

Today I’m back to talking about the Pacer Metaurus US Large Cap Dividend Multiplier 400 ETF (QDPL) with enthusiasm driven by 2 reasons:

  • a dividend 4x larger than the S&P 500

  • and a linear growth rate even in gloomy periods, like 2022…

I honestly think it could come in handy in a historical period where persistent inflation with sustained rates is expected and where the S&P 500’s earning yield remains at 3.59%.

Don’t you think? But before getting into the heart of the matter, for new readers I’ll take you through.

Read more here.

NextDecade: How Do You Like Me Now?

Fluidsdoc | Strong Buy

Clearly we picked the right horse last summer with our Strong Buy recommendation on Woodside Energy (WDS). Time and the market have been kind to us, and I think the best is yet to come for WDS. Events of this week and a nudge from the editors at Seeking Alpha had me looking at a sell recommendation I made last January on NextDecade (NEXT). A recommendation that has stood the test of time in terms of stock performance over the intervening period but has been overtaken by events as this new era of energy dawns. I've just used the term "New Era" for the first time and will expound on why I think this is the case, and particularly so for American LNG.

Read more here.

HydroGraph: Demand Scaling And The Defense Angle

Joffrey Simonet | Buy

This article is a follow-up to my first piece available here; I will thereafter assume you are familiar with it before reading this one. Today, I would like to cover and discuss the various significant developments that have happened since my previous article in November and give a qualitative assessment of how HydroGraph (HGRAF) is tracking against the expected execution timeline I laid out previously.

I will first summarize what those significant developments have been, and then this will lead me to discuss my two main themes for today: How HydroGraph should be (and is) scaling up the end demand for its products, and how they can leverage qualification into the U.S. Defense ecosystem as an accelerator for said demand. I alluded to that angle in my last piece, but there are now some tangible early signs that HG is executing on that front, which look promising in my view and deserve further discussion.

Read more here.

VIG Vs. VYM: Best Time In ~10 Years To Buy Dividend Growth

Sensor Unlimited | Strong Buy

My last coverage on the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG) was published on Feb 10. The analysis rated VIG as a buy after examining the sector ratio from tech-heavy indices to more value-oriented funds like VIG. As for the Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF Shares (VYM), I last covered it on Dec 16, 2026. That analysis also ended with a buy rating for VYM after benchmarking its growth-adjusted valuation against the SP500.

Since then, a few new catalysts have evolved both in terms of macroeconomic parameters and also fund specifics. In the former bucket, several new macroscopic parameters have changed, including the new inflation data, the Fed’s latest dot-plot, and also the potential of higher inflation driven by the conflict in the Middle East. Overall, these changes have led the market to form a more hawkish interest outlook. Given VIG and VYM’s strong bond-proxy flavor, the prices of VYM and VIG have corrected substantially since my last writings, calling for a reevaluation.

Read more here.

Wheaton Precious Metals: Gift Of The Cartel

Trapping Value | Hold

On our previous coverage of Wheaton Precious Metals Corp. (WPM), we looked at the biggest FOMO deal of the century. Our opinion was that WPM was buying things at a ridiculous price, and things won't work out. We felt that BHP Group Limited (BHP) did get an excellent deal for the resources that it forked over. That was the rationale for the sell rating that we stamped on the WPM stock. Since then, WPM has cratered, and so have its brethren. We go over three reasons why the math is less compelling to keep a Sell on this.

Read more here.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Adyen Seems Like A Bargain Again
data.description:
It has only been four months since I published my last article about Adyen N.V. (ADYEY) in mid-November 2025. In the article I had difficulties reaching a clear conclusion and wrote that the Dutch payment company is a rather complicated story and caught between high-growth potential and a potential stock turnaround on the one hand and the risk of an economy slowing down and high-growth rates on the other hand. In the conclusion I wrote:

It is hard for me to make up my mind about Adyen. On the one hand, the stock actually seems cheap and must grow only 14% annually for the next ten years to be fairly valued right now. This is clearly below the growth rates Adyen reported in the past and it is clearly below the growth rates analysts are expecting for the years to come.

On the other hand, growth rates of 14% for at least ten years can be almost impossible to achieve for a business if the economy is struggling and consumers decrease spending drastically during a recession. And although Adyen is still trading 50% below its previous all-time highs, the stock is still trading at high valuation multiples. I personally don’t have a position in Adyen and therefore I don’t have to make up my mind – I will just stay on the sidelines and rate Adyen as a “Hold” once again.

While I had difficulties making up my mind, investors obviously had a clear opinion about the company and especially about the stock. Since my last article was published, the stock declined from about €1,460 in mid-November to only €877 at the time of writing—resulting in a 40% decline in the stock. Adyen is also coming close again to its 2023 lows, with the stock now trading about 70% below its previous all-time lows.

In the following article, I will argue why Adyen is cheap enough to be a buy and why several risks for the business should already be reflected in the stock price at this point.

Performing in Line With Peers

While a decline of 70% seems steep when considering that the S&P 500 is less than 10% below its previous all-time high, Adyen is certainly not the only company from the Transaction & Payment Processing Services Sector declining steeply. The two heavy-weights – Mastercard (MA) and Visa (V)—are keeping up quite well, and both declined between 15% and 20%.

Both, especially the “younger” companies, were hit hard, and stocks like Block (XYZ), Fiserv (FISV), or PayPal (PYPL) all declined at least 60% from their previous all-time highs. PayPal was already hit hard in 2022; other stocks like Fiserv followed in 2025.

This information is important as we might be looking at a form of “collective punishment,” and some stocks might be punished only because the entire sector is selling off.

Valuation Multiple

It is possible that Adyen was mostly punished as investors sold off almost every stock from this industry for the last few years, but we should not ignore that Adyen was also trading for very high valuation multiples, and this often creates a risk of steep declines. In the following chart, I display the P/E and P/FCF ratios only for the last three years to make the data more readable. While the valuation multiples were even higher before, Adyen was still trading for almost 100 times earnings in 2023 or for 77 times free cash flow in 2024.

And such valuation multiples increase the risk for major setbacks and might also be part of the explanation for Adyen’s steep decline. Right now, the stock is still trading for 26 times earnings and for 30 times free cash flow, which are still high valuation multiples (but they might be justified if Adyen is able to continue growing in the double digits—we will get to this).

Fiscal 2025 Results

As mentioned above, high-growth rates are certainly necessary to justify the current valuation multiples, but so far the company is delivering these high-growth rates. When looking at the second half results, net revenue increased from €1,083 million in H2/24 to €1,271 million in H2/25, resulting in 17.4% top line growth for the business. And diluted net profit per share increased 11.6% year-over-year from €16.49 in H2/24 to €18.40 in H2/25.

For the full year 2025, growth rates are even higher. Net revenue increased from €1,996 million in fiscal 2024 to €2,364 million in fiscal 2025, resulting in 18.4% year-over-year top line growth. And diluted net income per share increased 13.6% year-over-year from €29.59 in fiscal 2024 to €33.61 in fiscal 2025.

High Growth Persisting?

Management also issued guidance for fiscal 2026 and is optimistic high-growth rates will persist. Revenue is expected to grow 20% to 22% year-over-year on a constant currency basis, which would be in line with fiscal 2025 (21% growth on a constant currency basis). And while the EBITDA margin in 2026 is expected to be similar to 2025, it is expected to improve to 55% by 2028. Finally, capital expenditures are expected to be around 5% of revenue once again.

During the last Investor Day (held in November 2025), management also presented the building blocks that will drive net revenue growth in the years to come. With different components contributing—like market volume growth, new wins, and share of wallet gains—management is expecting approximately 20% year-over-year revenue growth in the years to come.

Adyen is also optimistic it is currently operating in a market that is growing at a high pace, and its embedded financial products are expanding the market opportunity and the total addressable market. According to estimates, management is seeing its TAM around €127 billion stretched over Capital, Issuing, and Accounts.

When looking at analysts’ estimates for the years to come, they are obviously not sharing management’s optimism. Nevertheless, they are also expecting Adyen’s revenue to grow at a high pace in the years to come, and between fiscal 2025 and fiscal 2035, revenue is expected to grow with a CAGR of 14.4%.

And when looking at different studies, we also see high growth rates being expected. Boston Consulting Group is looking at transaction revenue and assumes it will grow in the mid-single digits, with the Middle East, Africa, and Latin America being the regions with the highest growth rates.

Other studies that are focusing especially on the payment processing solution market expect much higher growth rates (in line with Adyen’s management). Precedence Research is expecting this market to grow with a CAGR of 19.8% in the years until fiscal 2035, while Mordor Intelligence is expecting 18% annual growth for the years until fiscal 2031.

And although it is always difficult to assess this from the outside, it seems that Adyen is focusing on innovation and launching new products in a fast but responsible way. The company also seems to be at the forefront of innovation, and these are the requirements for growing in the double digits in the years to come.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

So far, we have established that Adyen has the potential to grow at a high pace in the years to come, and the high valuation multiples (see section above) might be justified. But as always, we are using a discounted cash flow calculation to determine an intrinsic value for the stock.

As always, we are calculating with a 10% discount rate (the annual return we like to achieve at least) as well as the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (31.54 million at the end of fiscal 2025). The basis of our calculation will be the free cash flow of fiscal 2025, which was €1,081.3 million. In a first calculation, we assume 20% growth for the next fiscal year—in line with management’s guidance—and assume growth will slow down over the next ten years until the company can grow only 4% until perpetuity (in line with the CFI’s recommendation for terminal growth rate). Calculating with these assumptions leads to an intrinsic value of €1,011, and Adyen is already a bit undervalued at this point.

In my opinion, these assumptions are rather cautious, and we might also calculate with slightly higher growth rates. Compared to the assumptions used above, management as well as analysts are more optimistic, and therefore, in a second calculation, I will use the growth rates analysts are estimating for the years to come (see section above) for the next ten years, followed by 4% growth until perpetuity. This leads to an intrinsic value of €1,172 for Adyen, and the stock would therefore trade more than 25% below its intrinsic value.

Risks

While there are reasons to be optimistic and assume double-digit growth rates for several years to come, we should not ignore the risks. And probably the biggest risk is growth rates slowing down much faster than expected at this point. Adyen management is still expecting growth rates of 20% for the next few years, but we should not ignore that estimates have constantly been revised to the downside in the last few quarters. In my first article about Adyen (published 2.5 years ago), management was still expecting revenue to grow in the mid-20s or maybe even low-30s. We clearly should not underestimate the risk of growth rates slowing down quickly—this has happened to other companies as well.

Slowing down growth rates could stem from several different reasons, but one big threat could be intensified competition. In such a scenario not only revenue growth might slow down, but margins might also compress—leading to lower growth rates quickly. And we should not forget that—despite being a service—payments are a commodity.

Counterarguments to the commodity-like nature of payments and Adyen getting under pressure are the facts that Adyen seems to be in a good position and a well-run company. It is still trying to hire personnel very selectively and grow at a responsible pace it can manage. As a result, the revenue per FTE was constantly growing in the last few years—from €376k in Q2/23 to €489k in Q3/25.

Another strong sign for Adyen being in a good position is the high return on invested capital the business is reporting in the last few years. This indicates pricing power and can therefore be seen as a counterargument to its services being only a commodity.

In the above-mentioned study by Boston Consulting Group, the authors are also writing that payment companies will have to focus more and more on value-added services. It estimates that almost half of revenue will stem from value-added services or embedded finance in 2027 in the United States (with Europe lagging a little bit behind in this trend)

This is also a way to fight the commodity-like nature of Adyen’s services. While payment processing itself is a commodity, value-added services can be a good way to differentiate themselves from their competitors.

A final risk for Adyen is the loss of one (or several) major customers, as the company has many of the biggest corporations using its services—including Uber, Spotify, eBay, McDonald's, and Starbucks. Despite having several large customers, Adyen’s top 10 customers accounted for only 11% of revenue in fiscal 2025 (in the previous year it was 12%). This is indicating that losing one single customer is probably not a major problem. Additionally, Adyen was able to diversify its business. Only four years ago, in 2021, the top 10 customers accounted for 29%, and in 2022 they accounted for 33%.

Conclusion

I am always a bit reluctant to invest in companies that require high-growth rates in a phase where it is unclear how long high-growth rates will prevail. In the case of Adyen, we are always facing the risk of growth rates slowing down faster than expected. This might lead to a scenario where Adyen is still overvalued—despite declining already 70% from its previous all-time high.

However, Adyen does not have to grow 20% in the next few years to be fairly valued right now, and in my opinion, the growth assumptions made in my calculation seem reasonable and already account for several headwinds. And while I would not rule out that Adyen will drop to its 2023 lows around €600 again, I see the stock already as a “Buy” at this point, and the stock should be a solid long-term investment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: HPI: This 9.7% Yielding Preferred Fund Is A Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

With market indices pulling back from their highs, it can be difficult to determine where the good opportunities are. However, the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund (HPI) now trades at an attractive valuation, so I wanted to reassess its overall value proposition. When I previously covered HPI, I issued a hold rating due to the limited growth potential related to leverage. Since then, the fund's share price has declined by more than 12.4%, and the total return has not kept up with the S&P 500 Index (SPX). However, the fund has released an updated report, and the interest rate environment has shifted. Therefore, I anticipate that performance will improve once we get over the current macroeconomic uncertainties.

Furthermore, the fund now trades at one of the most attractive ends of its historical range. For instance, HPI now trades at a very small premium to NAV of 0.26%. For reference, HPI has traded at an average premium to NAV of 2.47% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that the fund now trades at a more reasonable price-to-NAV range that may justify the accumulation of shares.

The fund now offers a dividend yield of 9.7%, and the fund continues to demonstrate its ability to generate earnings that exceed distributions. Therefore, HPI remains a great option for investors that want to prioritize a steady stream of income. Forward-looking, I believe that will substantially improve when interest rates trend lower and as the financial sector is capable of participating in the expansion of AI. So let's start by reviewing the underlying strategy that HPI implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, HPI now has total managed assets of $686.8M that are spread across 167 different holdings. What makes HPI unique is the focus on preferred stocks and preferred convertible securities, which helps put an emphasis on sustainable income generation. The fund also has an expense ratio of 1.2% of assets, which is high but a direct result of the active management within its portfolio. The turnover rate is typically around 35%, which means that management is constantly rotating into new positions to maintain healthy cash flows. According to the fact sheet, the fund's breakdown by asset class is as follows:

  • Preferred Securities: 56.25%
  • Corporate Bonds: 40.63%
  • U.S. Agency: 1.78%

Furthermore, the fund puts an emphasis on financial securities, and management specifically looks for securities that have strong cash flows and a high issuance of preferred shares. For instance, exposure to financials makes up 37.67% of assets. However, it's important to note that HPI maintains this exposure through income-focused securities and not through common equity. Therefore, the fund isn't structurally capable of fully participating in the upside growth of its own holdings.

When looking at the top holdings of the fund, we can see that the fund maintains exposure to many high-quality positions. For instance, Bank of America (BAC) is the largest holding at 6.05% of assets. This is followed by other notable positions like Citigroup (C), Morgan Stanley (MS), CMS Energy Corp. (CMS), Wells Fargo (WFC), and Citizens Financial Group (CFG), just to name a few. HPI also allocates most of its positions to securities with strong credit ratings. For reference, the breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 59.94%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 37.51%
  • Note Rated: 0.31%

Lastly, the fund tends to focus on long-dated maturities within its portfolio. For instance, 50.97% of assets are allocated to securities with a maturity above 30 years. However, many preferred stocks issued by banks don't always have a maturity date, so they are allocated to this 30-year category. The tradeoff is that longer-dated positions mean HPI will have a secure source of cash flow for many years to come. However, the longer-dated positions also mean that these positions are sensitive to a high-interest-rate environment. A high-interest rate directly causes the value of these positions to decline, which is why HPI's growth potential may be limited as long as interest rates are elevated.

Attractive Outlook

The biggest challenge for HPI is to thrive in a higher interest rate environment. Despite several interest rate cuts through 2025, the federal funds rate remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. Even during the Fed's latest meeting, it was decided that rates would be left unchanged. As we can see below, the fund has struggled to regain enough positive momentum to get back to its pre-pandemic levels. I anticipate that lower interest rates will continue to allow upside growth in its share price as the underlying operating spread improves.

For instance, the fund uses leverage in an effort to expand its earnings when possible. For instance, total effective leverage amounts to 38.64% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$257.1M. I consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage while interest rates are elevated because it directly increases the risk profile of the fund. For instance, a high-interest rate will negatively impact the spread between the cost of its leverage and the yield collected from securities. When interest rates eventually trend lower, the spread becomes wider and leads to higher earnings.

When reviewing the latest semi-annual report for 2026, we get some more detailed insights into the fund's underlying earnings structure. For instance, net investment income amounted to $0.56 per share, which is on track to land within its usual range according to prior years. This is an indication that the portfolio strategy has a very consistent level of income generation through different market cycles. However, you may notice that net investment income was much higher during the 2021 and 2022 periods, likely due to the healthier operating spreads when interest rates were lower.

Furthermore, a lower interest rate environment can increase the odds of large net realized gains being captured. For instance, HPI was able to capture net realized gains of $2.47 per share back in 2021 when interest rates were at near-zero levels. Assuming a similar performance through the rest of the 2026 period, HPI is on track to deliver positive net realized gains. However, the dependence on interest rates has impacted the rate of NAV growth over the last few years. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $20.81 per share
  • 2022: $17.99 per share
  • 2023: $15.31 per share
  • 2024: $16.13 per share
  • 2025: $16.02 per share
  • 2026 (semi-annual): $16.10 per share

While the growth is traditionally linked to interest rates, I believe that HPI is becoming increasingly aligned to participate in the growth of AI. For instance, HPI still maintains exposure to utilities companies, such as Energy Transfer (ET), NextEra Energy (NEE), and Entergy Corp. (ETR). These companies essentially provide exposure to the picks and shovels of the AI infrastructure market and are directly aligned to benefit from the large capex spending of the hyperscalers.

Grand View Research estimates that the size of the AI data center market can grow to $810.6B by 2033. This represents a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 23.9% over the next few years. Despite the exposure through preferred shares, the fund can still see some upside growth if it holds convertible securities but still provides a better risk-adjusted approach to the exposure. For instance, here are some of the positive metrics that HPI's holdings are already experiencing:

  • Entergy Corp: management estimates an 8% annual EPS growth with a $43B capital plan as data center demand surges.
  • NextEra Energy: expects up to 30GW of power for data centers by 2035.

Risks

Although I am optimistic about the outlook of the fund, there are some headwinds that investors should be aware of. The biggest risk is if interest rate cuts are delayed or they trend higher over the next twelve months. Since the majority of the portfolio is allocated to securities with 30-year maturities, an uptick in rates will cause values within HPI to decline. In this scenario, leverage will continue to make it difficult for earnings to improve going forward.

Furthermore, I want to emphasize that HPI only makes sense for investors that want to specifically focus on dividends over time. HPI isn't structurally built to fully participate in the upside growth of its holdings, so long-term performance may not be as attractive as a standard index-tracking ETF, such as the State Street Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF). A comparison of the two funds makes it very clear that a high dividend yield doesn't automatically translate to higher returns.

For instance, HPI's ten-year total return sits at 54.6%, which severely underperforms against XLF's total return of 216.8% over the same time frame. As we can see, HPI's share price has also eroded over the last ten years due to the impacts of leverage, higher rates, and occasionally paying out more than it earns. So if you want to prioritize high returns, a traditional approach ETF may be more suitable.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1235 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 9.7%. If we multiply this monthly payout by twelve months, we get an estimated annual distribution of $1.482 per share. Referring back to the same semi-annual report, we can see that the fund has historically generated more income than it distributed to shareholders. Furthermore, the fund has displayed that it is capable of generating earnings that can support several years' worth of payouts in the past. For instance, his was demonstrated back when interest rates were low in 2021:

2021's earnings of $3.86 per share / $1.482 annual payout = 2.6 years of coverage.

So when interest rates trend lower and the fund is in a better environment to thrive, I anticipate that HPI's coverage will become much stronger. The payout history does reveal that payouts were lowered at the end of 2019 and haven't recovered yet. However, I believe that management is making the right choice by not raising the dividends yet because it allows the NAV to remain stable over time. Despite the lack of dividend raises, the consistent dividend levels over the last decade mean that long-term holders are capable of creating their own dividend growth by reinvesting distributions or adding new capital over time.

To demonstrate this concept, I ran a backtest on an initial $100,000 investment at the start of 2017. This backtest assumes that no additional capital was invested after the initial $100k. However, it does assume that all dividends received were reinvested back into the accumulation of shares. By simply remaining invested and staying patient, investors would have seen their annual dividend income increase over the last few years.

2017 Dividend Income: $8,562

2025 Dividend Income: $14,546

Lastly, investors need to consider the fact that distributions received from HPI may not offer any tax efficiency for investors. For instance, the latest available Section 19(a) notice indicates that 77% of the distributions paid YTD were classified as net investment income. This means that most of the distributions would be classified as ordinary income, which has the highest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in HPI would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to limit their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that HPI is now an attractive buy based on its valuation and attractive outlook over the next twelve months. In a scenario where interest rates continue to trend downward, this will improve the fund's operating spread and encourage higher valuations across its portfolio. Furthermore, the portfolio is aligned to capture some of the growth of AI infrastructure through its utilities exposure. The reporting indicates that the portfolio is certainly capable of generating earnings that far exceed the distributions that are being paid. Lastly, the fund remains a great option for investors seeking a reliable source of income generation over time.
